abstract:
This study investigates the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-β-IEPOX onto hydrated inorganic seed particles in the absence of added acids. While no organic aerosol (OA) formation occurred on dry ammonium sulfate (AS), both isomers exhibited prompt and efficient OA growth on AS seeds with liquid water contents ranging from 40% to 75% by mass. This OA formation is identified as a kinetically limited process, where growth persists as long as a gas-phase IEPOX reservoir is available. Notably, no significant differences in growth rates or chemical composition were observed between the cis and trans isomers.

High OA loadings were also achieved on hydrated AS seeds treated with a base (NaOH), suggesting that the pH dependence of IEPOX reactive uptake is relatively weak for ammonium-containing particles. In contrast, no residual OA was detected after drying when Na⁺ was substituted for NH₄⁺. Using NaCl particles at an ionic strength of ~9 M, the Henry’s Law partitioning coefficient for IEPOX was determined to be 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹ (−50 / +100%). When NH₄⁺ was present but sulfate was replaced by chloride, only minimal OA was produced, highlighting the potential role of nucleophilic strength in these reactions. 

Chemical analysis via online aerosol mass spectrometry and offline filter extracts revealed a composition rich in oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates, and amines. These findings align with field observations of weak correlations between IEPOX-derived OA and particle acidity or liquid water, suggesting that in many atmospheric environments, the system is limited by nucleophile availability rather than water content or catalytic activity.
